Course Title: Connected Vehicles and Expanding Attack Surfaces
Category: Transportation & Logistics: Aviation, Maritime, Logistics, Shipping, Automotive
Description: IoT and AI increasing exposure in transport ecosystems.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with networking concepts and a general interest in how modern vehicles and transport systems connect to digital infrastructure are all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key vulnerabilities introduced by IoT and AI technologies across connected vehicle ecosystems and explain how expanding attack surfaces affect security in automotive, aviation, and maritime transport. You will also apply foundational cybersecurity principles to recognise potential threats within transport and logistics networks, helping you to better understand how organisations can begin to protect their connected infrastructure.

lessons.lesson title: Connected Vehicles and Expanding Attack Surfaces
lessons.Description: Modern vehicles are no longer simply mechanical machines. They are increasingly connected devices that communicate with other vehicles, infrastructure, and cloud-based services, creating a vast and complex digital ecosystem. This lesson introduces the concept of attack surfaces and explores how the integration of IoT technologies and AI-driven systems into transport networks has significantly expanded the opportunities for cyber threats. You will learn how everyday features such as GPS navigation, remote diagnostics, and over-the-air software updates can introduce vulnerabilities if not properly secured. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of why cybersecurity is now a critical consideration for anyone working with or around connected transport systems.
lessons.content: IoT and AI increasing exposure in transport ecosystems.

slides.subtitle: How vehicles became connected computers
slides.slidecontent:
• Vehicles evolved from mechanical machines to connected computers
• GPS, diagnostics, and software updates create digital touchpoints
• Every connection opens potential security vulnerabilities
• Transport networks now mirror IT infrastructure risks
• Cybersecurity becomes critical for physical safety

slides.subtitle: Defining vulnerability in connected systems
slides.slidecontent:
• Attack surface: all possible entry points for cyber threats
• Each new feature adds potential vulnerabilities
• IoT sensors and communication interfaces multiply exposure
• Connected components create cascading risk pathways
• Security failures can impact vehicle safety systems

slides.subtitle: Mapping the connected ecosystem components
slides.slidecontent:
• Sensors, cameras, and radars collecting environmental data
• Wireless interfaces: Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular, DSRC
• Internal networks linking ECU and infotainment systems
• Telematics units connecting to cloud platforms
• Each component represents a potential entry point

slides.subtitle: How wireless connectivity creates security risks
slides.slidecontent:
• Relay attacks bypass keyless entry security
• Intercepted over-the-air updates can deliver malicious code
• Mobile app vulnerabilities expose authentication tokens
• Compromised wireless access can affect steering and braking
• Remote exploits threaten core vehicle functions

slides.subtitle: Signal interception and network exploitation
slides.slidecontent:
• Multiple wireless protocols create diverse attack opportunities
• Signal amplification defeats proximity-based security
• Weak encryption exposes entire vehicle networks
• Infotainment systems share networks with safety-critical components
• Cross-system contamination amplifies single-point failures

slides.subtitle: Third-party vulnerabilities in vehicle components
slides.slidecontent:
• Dozens of suppliers contribute software and hardware components
• Single supplier vulnerability affects all vehicles using their parts
• Limited manufacturer visibility into supplier security practices
• Malicious updates can propagate through supplier relationships
• Complex dependency chains obscure security assessment

slides.subtitle: New technologies expanding vulnerability landscape
slides.slidecontent:
• AI systems introduce algorithmic manipulation risks
• IoT devices multiply network endpoints exponentially
• Machine learning models vulnerable to adversarial attacks
• Autonomous features depend on external data sources
• Smart infrastructure creates city-wide attack vectors

slides.subtitle: Essential practices for connected transport security
slides.slidecontent:
• Regular updates and patch management
• Strong authentication and access controls
• Network monitoring and anomaly detection
• Assume every endpoint is a potential target
• Multi-layered defense strategies for transport systems

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on connected vehicles and expanding attack surfaces. The transportation industry has undergone a fundamental transformation. What were once purely mechanical machines have evolved into sophisticated connected computers on wheels, rails, and water. Modern vehicles now feature GPS navigation, remote diagnostics, and over-the-air software updates as standard features. Each of these conveniences creates digital touchpoints that can be exploited if not properly secured. Unlike traditional IT systems, vehicle security failures don't just risk data breaches—they can directly threaten physical safety. This shift means cybersecurity is no longer optional in transportation; it's become a critical safety requirement that affects everyone who uses or works with modern transport systems.

To understand the security challenges facing connected vehicles, we need to understand what cybersecurity professionals mean by 'attack surface.' This term describes all the possible ways an attacker could try to gain unauthorized access to a system. In traditional vehicles, this surface was minimal—mostly physical access points. But as vehicles have become connected, this surface has expanded dramatically. Every new IoT sensor, every wireless communication interface, every software feature adds potential entry points. What makes this particularly concerning in transportation is that these systems are interconnected. A vulnerability in one component can provide access to others, creating cascading risks that can ultimately affect critical safety systems like braking or steering.

Let's map out what a modern connected vehicle actually contains from a security perspective. You'll find dozens of sensors, cameras, and radar systems continuously collecting data about the vehicle and its environment. These systems communicate through various wireless interfaces—Bluetooth for device pairing, Wi-Fi for internet access, cellular networks for remote services, and dedicated short-range communications for vehicle-to-vehicle messaging. Internally, networks connect the engine control unit, infotainment systems, and other electronic components. Telematics units constantly transmit vehicle data to manufacturer cloud platforms for analysis and service delivery. Each of these components represents a potential entry point, and understanding this architecture is crucial for identifying where vulnerabilities might exist.

Remote access capabilities have introduced some of the most serious security risks in modern vehicles. Keyless entry systems, while convenient, are vulnerable to relay attacks where criminals use signal amplifiers to trick vehicles into unlocking from a distance. Over-the-air software updates, if not properly encrypted and authenticated, can become channels for delivering malicious code directly to vehicle systems. Mobile applications that control vehicle functions often store authentication credentials insecurely, potentially giving attackers full remote access if devices are compromised. Most alarmingly, successful remote exploits can potentially affect core driving functions including steering, acceleration, and braking systems, turning cybersecurity vulnerabilities into immediate safety threats.

The wireless communication protocols that make vehicles convenient also create multiple pathways for attack. Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and cellular connections each use different security approaches, and weaknesses in any one protocol can be exploited. Signal amplification attacks can defeat proximity-based security measures, making 'short-range' connections accessible from much greater distances. A critical concern is that many vehicle architectures share network infrastructure between entertainment systems and safety-critical components. This means that weak encryption or poor network segmentation in one system can expose the entire vehicle ecosystem. Attackers often target the easiest entry point and then move laterally through connected systems to reach more valuable targets.

One of the most complex security challenges in connected vehicles comes from the supply chain itself. Modern vehicles integrate software and hardware components from dozens of different suppliers, each with their own security practices and standards. The problem is that vehicle manufacturers often have limited visibility into their suppliers' actual security implementations. A single vulnerable component from one supplier can potentially compromise every vehicle that incorporates it. This risk is amplified by over-the-air update mechanisms—if these channels aren't properly secured, they could potentially be exploited to distribute malicious code through supplier relationships. The interconnected nature of modern vehicle production means that security is only as strong as the weakest link in a very complex chain.

The integration of artificial intelligence and expanded IoT connectivity is creating entirely new categories of security challenges. AI systems in vehicles can be vulnerable to adversarial attacks that manipulate their decision-making processes, potentially affecting autonomous driving features or predictive maintenance systems. IoT proliferation means vehicles now connect to smart traffic lights, road sensors, and city-wide infrastructure networks, exponentially multiplying potential attack vectors. Machine learning models depend on external data sources that could be compromised or manipulated. As vehicles become more autonomous and cities develop smart infrastructure, we're creating interconnected systems where a security breach in one area could cascade across entire transportation networks, affecting multiple vehicles and infrastructure simultaneously.

Protecting connected transport systems requires applying foundational cybersecurity principles consistently across all components. Regular updates and comprehensive patch management are essential—security vulnerabilities are discovered constantly, and leaving systems unpatched creates unnecessary risk. Strong authentication and access controls help ensure only authorized users and systems can interact with vehicle networks. Continuous network monitoring and anomaly detection can identify suspicious activity before it leads to successful attacks. It's crucial to assume that every connected endpoint—whether it's a sensor, communication interface, or software component—could potentially be targeted by attackers. The most effective approach is implementing multi-layered defense strategies that don't rely on any single security measure, creating resilient systems that can maintain safety even when individual components are compromised.

Who is this for: Fleet managers, logistics coordinators, transport operations staff, automotive technicians, aviation maintenance personnel, maritime operations officers, supply chain managers, vehicle procurement specialists
Team or Dept this is for: Transportation, Logistics, Operations, Fleet Management, Supply Chain, Maintenance, Procurement
